channel.title: Commodore 64 on rowstu.net
channel.description: Recent content in Commodore 64 on rowstu.net

item.title: Commodore BASIC Programs to Try
item.description:
The C64 boots straight into Commodore BASIC V2. It’s limited compared to later BASICs (no commands for graphics or sound — you poke memory directly), but that’s part of the charm. Here are some programs to type in, from trivial to genuinely useful.

The Essentials

Hello World

10 PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
20 GOTO 10
RUN

The simplest infinite loop. Press RUN/STOP to break out.

The Maze

The most famous C64 one-liner. Prints a random maze using PETSCII diagonal characters.

item.title: Connecting Your C64 to BBSes
item.description:
Bulletin Board Systems are still running in 2024, and connecting from a real C64 is one of the most satisfying retro computing experiences. Most modern BBSes accept telnet connections, and a cheap WiFi modem makes this trivially easy.

What You Need

  1. A C64 (any model)
  2. A WiFi modem — an ESP8266 or ESP32-based device that connects to the C64’s user port and provides a Hayes AT command interface over WiFi
  3. A terminal program on the C64
  4. Your WiFi network credentials

WiFi Modems

These are small boards that plug into the C64 user port and emulate a standard Hayes-compatible modem. Internally they connect to your WiFi and translate AT commands into TCP/telnet connections.

item.title: File Transfer and Disk Images on the C64
item.description:
Getting software onto a real C64 from a modern PC is a common task. This covers the file formats, tools, and workflows for moving data between the two worlds.

File Formats

Format Extension What it is
D64 .d64 Disk image of a 1541 floppy (170KB, 35 tracks). The most common format
D71 .d71 Disk image of a 1571 floppy (340KB, double-sided)
D81 .d81 Disk image of a 1581 floppy (800KB, 3.5" disk)
G64 .g64 Low-level GCR disk image. Preserves copy protection and non-standard tracks
PRG .prg A single C64 program file. First two bytes are the load address
CRT .crt Cartridge ROM image. Used by cartridge-port devices (EasyFlash, Kung Fu Flash)
T64 .t64 Tape image. Emulates a datasette recording
TAP .tap Raw tape image with pulse timing data. More accurate than T64
SID .sid SID music file. Playable in SID players (HVSC format)
SEQ .seq Sequential file (text/data)
REL .rel Relative file (random access)

For day-to-day use, you’ll mostly deal with D64 and PRG files.

item.title: JiffyDOS – Speed Up Your C64 Disk Access
item.description:
JiffyDOS is a replacement ROM for the C64 (and 1541/1571/1581 drives) that dramatically speeds up disk access and adds a set of useful DOS wedge commands. It was originally sold in the late 1980s and is still available as a licensed product.

What It Does

The standard C64-to-1541 serial bus transfer rate is around 300 bytes/second — painfully slow. JiffyDOS replaces the serial protocol with a faster byte transfer routine, achieving roughly 10x speed improvement.

item.title: Modern Hardware for the Commodore 64
item.description:
The C64 scene is alive and well. A whole ecosystem of modern hardware exists to keep these machines useful and enjoyable. This is a rundown of the most important upgrades.

Video Output

The original C64 outputs composite and S-Video (via the 8-pin DIN). Getting a clean picture on a modern display is the first problem to solve.

Options

Device What it does Notes
RetroTINK 2X / 5X Analogue-to-HDMI upscaler The gold standard. Low latency, excellent picture. 5X does 1080p with scanline options
S-Video to HDMI adapter Cheap Amazon/eBay boxes Variable quality. The C64’s signal is non-standard enough that many don’t work well
Commodore monitor (1702/1084) Original CRT Best picture if you can find one. S-Video input gives a sharp image
LumaFix64 Internal mod Fixes the vertical jail bar interference on later C64C boards (8580 SID machines)
C64 RF modulator bypass Simple mod Bypasses the RF modulator for cleaner composite output

Recommendation: S-Video cable + RetroTINK for modern TVs, or a 1084S monitor if you want the authentic look.
